question_text: What is the maximum Pell Grant I can get for the 2025-26 school year?
ground_truth_answer: The maximum Federal Pell Grant award is $7,395 for the 2025-26 award year.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 7 Ch. 2 — Calculating Pell Grants

question_text: When is the last day I can submit my FAFSA for the 2025-26 school year?
ground_truth_answer: The 2025-26 FAFSA form must be submitted by 11:59 p.m. Central time (CT) on June 30, 2026.
section_reference: studentaid.gov — Federal Deadlines 2025-26

question_text: How many years can I receive a Pell Grant in total?
ground_truth_answer: You can receive the Pell Grant for no more than 12 semesters (or the equivalent), which is measured by your Lifetime Eligibility Used (LEU). Once you reach 100% LEU you are no longer eligible.
section_reference: studentaid.gov — Pell Grant overview (LEU limit)

question_text: Do I have to pay back a Pell Grant?
ground_truth_answer: Pell Grants do not have to be repaid in most cases. They are grants, not loans.
section_reference: studentaid.gov — Pell Grant overview

question_text: What GPA do I need to keep my federal financial aid?
ground_truth_answer: Students must maintain a minimum cumulative GPA of 2.0 on a 4.0 scale (or its equivalent) to maintain Satisfactory Academic Progress for federal aid eligibility.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP qualitative measure

question_text: How much of my attempted credits do I need to complete to keep my federal financial aid?
ground_truth_answer: Students must successfully complete at least 67% of all credit hours they attempt cumulatively.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP quantitative measure (pace)

question_text: Can I get financial aid if I'm enrolled less than half-time or only part-time?
ground_truth_answer: Students enrolled less than half-time may still receive a Pell Grant but are generally not eligible for subsidized or unsubsidized Direct Loans. Half-time enrollment is typically 6 credit hours per semester for undergraduates.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 7 Ch. 3 — Pell Grant enrollment intensity and COA

question_text: What is the most I can borrow in federal loans as a first-year undergraduate?
ground_truth_answer: First-year dependent undergraduate students may borrow up to $5,500 per year in Direct Loans (no more than $3,500 of which may be subsidized). First-year independent undergraduates may borrow up to $9,500 (no more than $3,500 subsidized).
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 3 Ch. 3 — Annual loan limits

question_text: What is the most I can ever borrow in federal loans as an undergraduate student?
ground_truth_answer: The aggregate loan limit for dependent undergraduates is $31,000 (no more than $23,000 subsidized). For independent undergraduates the limit is $57,500 (no more than $23,000 subsidized).
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 3 Ch. 3 — Aggregate loan limits

question_text: Does my parents' income affect my financial aid even if I pay for school myself?
ground_truth_answer: For dependent students, parental income and assets are included in the SAI calculation regardless of whether the parent contributes financially. A student is considered dependent unless they meet specific federal independence criteria.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 3 — SAI calculation, dependency status

question_text: What happens to my financial aid if I repeat a class I already passed?
ground_truth_answer: A student may receive Title IV aid for one repetition of a previously passed course. Aid cannot be paid for a second or subsequent repetition of a course the student passed.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — Repeated coursework policy

question_text: Can I get federal financial aid if I owe money on a previous federal loan that I haven't paid back?
ground_truth_answer: Students who are in default on a federal student loan are not eligible for additional Title IV federal student aid until the default is resolved.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 3 — NSLDS financial aid history and default status

question_text: How long do I have after I graduate before I have to start paying back my federal loans?
ground_truth_answer: For most federal student loan types you have a six-month grace period after you graduate, leave school, or drop below half-time enrollment before you must begin making payments.
section_reference: studentaid.gov — Subsidized and unsubsidized loans grace period

question_text: What is Federal Work-Study?
ground_truth_answer: Federal Work-Study provides part-time jobs for undergraduate and graduate students with financial need, allowing them to earn money to help pay education expenses. The program encourages community service work and work related to the student's course of study.
section_reference: studentaid.gov — Work-Study overview

question_text: Does getting a scholarship affect how much federal aid I can receive?
ground_truth_answer: Outside scholarships may reduce a student's financial need and therefore reduce need-based aid. Schools are required to adjust a student's aid package if total aid including outside scholarships exceeds the student's Cost of Attendance or financial need.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 3 Ch. 3 — Packaging and outside scholarships

question_text: If I take out a subsidized loan, do I have to pay interest while I'm still in school?
ground_truth_answer: No. The federal government pays the interest on Direct Subsidized Loans while the student is enrolled at least half-time. Interest does not accrue during this period.
section_reference: studentaid.gov loan types + FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 3 Ch. 1 — interest provisions

question_text: Can I get a Pell Grant and a Work-Study job at the same time?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. Pell Grants and Federal Work-Study are separate programs and can both be awarded in the same aid package. Total aid cannot exceed the student's Cost of Attendance.
section_reference: studentaid.gov Pell Grant overview + Work-Study overview

question_text: I'm going to school half-time instead of full-time — does that change how much Pell Grant I get?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. Pell Grant awards are adjusted based on enrollment intensity. A half-time student receives a proportionally reduced Pell Grant compared to a full-time student. The exact amount depends on the enrollment intensity percentage applied to the student's full-time award.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 7 Ch. 3 — enrollment intensity + Vol. AVG Ch. 3 — SAI and Pell eligibility

question_text: If I transfer to a different school, do I have to reapply for financial aid?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. Financial aid does not automatically transfer between schools. You must update your FAFSA to list the new school and the new institution will determine your aid eligibility based on its own Cost of Attendance and policies.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 1 — application process + Ch. 2 — updating FAFSA

question_text: Can I get financial aid for summer classes?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. Pell Grants can be awarded for summer enrollment if the student has remaining LEU and the school has a summer aid program. Direct Loans may also be available for summer if annual limits have not been reached. Summer aid depends on the school's academic calendar structure.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 7 Ch. 3 — Pell enrollment intensity + Vol. 3 Ch. 1 — academic year and payment periods

question_text: My parents refuse to help pay for college — does that mean I'm considered independent for financial aid?
ground_truth_answer: No. Parental refusal to pay does not make a student independent for Title IV purposes. Independence is determined by federal criteria including age, marital status, military service, and other factors — not by parental willingness to contribute.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 2 — dependency questions on FAFSA + Ch. 5 — special cases, dependency overrides

question_text: What is the difference between a subsidized and an unsubsidized loan?
ground_truth_answer: The government pays the interest on subsidized loans while the student is enrolled at least half-time; unsubsidized loans accrue interest from the time they are disbursed. Both have the same repayment terms otherwise. Eligibility for subsidized loans requires demonstrated financial need; unsubsidized loans are available regardless of need.
section_reference: studentaid.gov — subsidized/unsubsidized loan comparison + FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 3 Ch. 3 — need-based vs non-need-based packaging

question_text: Can I lose my financial aid for failing too many classes even if my GPA is still above 2.0?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. SAP has both a qualitative (GPA) and a quantitative (pace) standard. A student who passes fewer than 67% of attempted credits fails the pace standard and loses aid eligibility even if their GPA remains above 2.0.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP quantitative pace standard + studentaid.gov SAP consumer page

question_text: Does my financial aid get affected if I change my major?
ground_truth_answer: Changing a major does not directly affect federal aid eligibility, but it can affect the maximum timeframe standard. Students must complete their program within 150% of the published program length. A major change that adds required credits may push a student closer to the 150% limit.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP maximum timeframe + Vol. AVG Ch. 2 — enrollment reporting

question_text: What happens to my financial aid if I take a semester off?
ground_truth_answer: Taking a leave of absence may trigger the grace period on federal loans and affect SAP status depending on how the institution counts the leave. Pell Grant eligibility resumes when the student re-enrolls. The student must re-establish eligibility if they were placed on SAP suspension.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP and leaves of absence + studentaid.gov — loan grace period

question_text: Can a graduate student get a Pell Grant?
ground_truth_answer: No. Federal Pell Grants are available only to undergraduate students who have not yet earned a bachelor's or professional degree. Graduate students are not eligible.
section_reference: studentaid.gov Pell Grant overview + FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 7 Ch. 1 — Pell Grant student eligibility

question_text: How do I know if I qualify for a subsidized loan versus an unsubsidized loan?
ground_truth_answer: Subsidized loan eligibility requires demonstrated financial need (COA minus SAI greater than zero) and enrollment at least half-time. Unsubsidized loans are available to eligible students regardless of financial need. Both require the student to be enrolled at least half-time in a degree or certificate program.
section_reference: studentaid.gov — subsidized loans + FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 3 — SAI and need determination

question_text: If my family's financial situation gets worse after I submit my FAFSA, can I get more aid?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. Students or families who experience significant changes in financial circumstances after filing the FAFSA can contact their school's financial aid office to request a professional judgment review. The aid administrator may adjust the SAI or Cost of Attendance based on documented special circumstances.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 5 — special circumstances + Ch. 2 — FAFSA corrections and updates

question_text: Can I get financial aid if I already have a bachelor's degree and I'm going back for a second one?
ground_truth_answer: Generally no for Pell Grants — Pell Grant eligibility ends once a student has earned a bachelor's degree. Direct Loans may still be available depending on aggregate limits and program eligibility. Work-Study may also be available.
section_reference: studentaid.gov Pell Grant overview + FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 7 Ch. 1 — Pell eligibility and prior degrees

question_text: Will getting married change how much financial aid I get?
ground_truth_answer: Yes, for independent students. Marriage changes dependency status — a married student is considered independent regardless of age. This means parental income is no longer included in the SAI calculation, which can significantly change aid eligibility.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 2 — FAFSA dependency questions + Ch. 3 — SAI calculation for independent students

question_text: Can I get a Pell Grant, a Work-Study job, and a loan all at the same time — and is there a limit to how much I can get total?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. All three can be awarded simultaneously. Total need-based aid cannot exceed the student's financial need (COA minus SAI). Total aid from all sources cannot exceed the Cost of Attendance.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 3 Ch. 3 — packaging + studentaid.gov Pell + Work-Study overviews

question_text: What happens to my loans and scholarship if I drop below half-time or withdraw from school?
ground_truth_answer: Federal Direct Loan grace periods begin. Subsidized loan interest starts accruing after the grace period ends. Return of Title IV funds calculations may require repayment of a portion of aid. University scholarship eligibility depends on institutional enrollment requirements.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 5 Ch. 1 — R2T4 + studentaid.gov loans + institutional policy

question_text: I'm the first in my family to go to college and my parents don't file taxes — how does that affect my FAFSA and my aid?
ground_truth_answer: Non-tax-filers can still complete the FAFSA. The FA-DDX will attempt to pull IRS data; if none exists, the student may need to provide additional documentation. Aid eligibility is based on the SAI calculated from reported income. First-generation students may also be eligible for TRIO and other support programs.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 2 — FAFSA non-filers + Ch. 5 — special cases + NASFAA consumer guide

question_text: Can I appeal if my financial aid gets cut off because of my grades?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. Students who fail to meet SAP standards may appeal by submitting documentation of mitigating circumstances (illness, family emergency, etc.) and an academic plan showing how they will meet SAP by a specified point. If approved, the student is placed on financial aid probation. The appeal and probation process is governed by institutional policy within federal guidelines.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP appeals and probation + studentaid.gov SAP consumer page + institutional SAP appeal policy

question_text: How is the amount of my financial aid package decided — who decides what I get?
ground_truth_answer: The school's financial aid office determines the aid package. It starts with the Cost of Attendance (set by the school), subtracts the SAI (calculated from FAFSA data), and packages aid up to the resulting financial need using available federal, state, and institutional funds, subject to program limits.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 3 Ch. 2 — COA + Ch. 3 — packaging + Vol. AVG Ch. 3 — SAI

question_text: My financial aid refund check is late — when is the school supposed to pay me and what are my rights?
ground_truth_answer: Schools must disburse Title IV aid within specific timeframes and must provide credit balance refunds to students within 14 days of the credit balance occurring. Students have the right to hold credit balances at the institution or have them returned.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 3 Ch. 1 — disbursement timing + Vol. 2 Ch. 3 — institutional Title IV admin requirements + Vol. AVG Ch. 4 — verification and disbursement holds

question_text: Can I use financial aid to pay for an online degree program?
ground_truth_answer: Yes, if the institution and program are Title IV eligible. Online programs at accredited institutions can qualify for Pell Grants, Direct Loans, and Work-Study. Distance education programs must meet specific requirements around regular and substantive interaction.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 2 Ch. 1 — institutional eligibility for distance education + Vol. 3 Ch. 3 — packaging + studentaid.gov Pell overview

question_text: I got more financial aid than I needed and spent it — do I have to pay any of it back if I withdraw?
ground_truth_answer: Yes, potentially. If a student withdraws before completing 60% of the payment period, a Return of Title IV Funds (R2T4) calculation determines how much aid was earned. Unearned aid must be returned by the school and possibly by the student. The order of return follows federal priority: unsubsidized loans, subsidized loans, Pell Grants, other grants.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 5 Ch. 1 — R2T4 calculation + Vol. 3 Ch. 1 — disbursement + Vol. AVG Ch. 4 — verification holds

question_text: My school put me on academic probation — is that the same as losing my financial aid?
ground_truth_answer: Not necessarily. Academic probation and financial aid SAP suspension are separate processes. A student can be on academic probation without losing federal aid if they still meet SAP standards. Conversely, a student can lose aid for failing SAP even if not on academic probation. The school determines both independently.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP independence from academic standing + studentaid.gov SAP consumer page + Pace University SAP policy

question_text: I'm getting married and my spouse has a lot of student loan debt — will that affect my financial aid?
ground_truth_answer: For independent students, a spouse's income is included in the SAI calculation, which can reduce aid eligibility. However, a spouse's existing student loan debt does not directly reduce the SAI. The FAFSA collects household income and assets, not existing debt obligations.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 2 — FAFSA spouse questions + Ch. 3 — SAI calculation + Vol. 3 Ch. 2 — COA and budget

question_text: Does the government require me to pass 80% of my classes to keep my financial aid?
ground_truth_answer: No. The federal SAP standard requires students to complete at least 67% of attempted credits cumulatively. An 80% requirement is above the federal floor and reflects institutional policy at some schools, such as the University of Washington.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP quantitative standard + UW SAP policy
contradiction_notes: UW verbatim: 'Complete a minimum of 80% of the cumulative credits you attempted.' Federal standard is 67%.

question_text: Is a 2.5 GPA required by the federal government for students in education programs to keep their aid?
ground_truth_answer: No. The federal SAP GPA minimum is 2.0 for all undergraduates regardless of program. A 2.5 GPA requirement for education students reflects institutional policy, such as at Pace University's School of Education.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP qualitative GPA standard + Pace University SAP policy
contradiction_notes: Pace verbatim: 'Minimum Cumulative QPA: 2.50' for School of Education undergraduate students. Federal standard is 2.0.

question_text: My school says I need to complete 70% of my classes once I've taken more than 96 credits — is that a federal rule?
ground_truth_answer: No. The federal SAP pace standard is 67% for all students regardless of how many credits they have attempted. A tiered or higher pace standard is an institutional policy. Pace University applies a 70% completion standard to students who have attempted 96 or more credits.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP quantitative standard + Pace University tiered SAP policy
contradiction_notes: Pace verbatim: '96-180 credits attempted: Required Percentage Passed 70%.' Federal standard is 67% at all credit levels.

question_text: Does the federal government set a maximum time limit for how long I can take to finish my degree and still get aid?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. The federal maximum timeframe standard requires students to complete their program within 150% of the published program length (e.g., six years for a four-year degree). However, some institutions set stricter timeframes. This is a federal floor, not a ceiling.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — SAP maximum timeframe + UW SAP maximum timeframe policy
source_priority_note: federal-primary (priority 1) — institutional policies may be stricter
contradiction_notes: UW SAP page specifies maximum timeframe consistent with federal 150% standard but applies it to all aid including institutional.

question_text: My school told me I need a 2.0 GPA to keep my aid — but the financial aid page says something different than what an advisor told me. Which one is right?
ground_truth_answer: The federal minimum is 2.0. If an institutional page states a higher requirement, the institutional standard applies to institutional aid while federal aid follows the federal standard. If there is a conflict between verbal advice and a written policy, the written policy on the institutional financial aid page governs.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. 1 Ch. 1 — federal SAP GPA floor + U-M SAP policy
source_priority_note: federal-primary (priority 1) for federal aid; institutional (priority 4) for institutional aid
contradiction_notes: U-M SAP page states 2.0 GPA minimum for undergraduates consistent with federal standard; confirms federal and institutional standards can differ for different aid types.

question_text: How does the government figure out how much financial aid I need?
ground_truth_answer: Financial need is calculated as: Cost of Attendance (COA) minus Student Aid Index (SAI). The SAI replaced the Expected Family Contribution (EFC) formula starting with the 2024-25 award year under the FAFSA Simplification Act.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 3 — SAI + studentaid.gov how aid is calculated
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: Financial Need = COA − EFC (Expected Family Contribution). EFC calculated using federal needs analysis formula.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: Financial Need = COA − SAI (Student Aid Index). SAI replaced EFC under FAFSA Simplification Act. SAI can be negative (as low as −1500).

question_text: What tax information does the FAFSA use and where does it come from?
ground_truth_answer: The 2025-26 FAFSA uses 2023 federal tax return data. The data is transferred automatically through the FUTURE Act Direct Data Exchange (FA-DDX), which replaced the IRS Data Retrieval Tool (IRS-DRT).
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 2 — FA-DDX + 2024-25 Vol. AVG Ch. 2 — IRS-DRT
source_priority_note: federal-primary 2025-26 (priority 1) — 2024-25 handbook referenced IRS-DRT which is retired
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: Tax data transferred via IRS Data Retrieval Tool (IRS-DRT). Applicants could opt in to pull data from 2022 tax returns.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: IRS-DRT retired. Replaced by FA-DDX (FUTURE Act Direct Data Exchange). Data pulled automatically from 2023 tax returns with applicant consent through StudentAid.gov.

question_text: I heard the FAFSA got simpler recently — what actually changed?
ground_truth_answer: The FAFSA Simplification Act reduced the number of questions on the FAFSA, replaced the EFC with the SAI, changed how family size is determined, and introduced the FA-DDX for automatic tax data transfer. The simplified FAFSA first applied to the 2024-25 award year.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 1 — overview of FAFSA changes + Ch. 2 — simplified form
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: First year of simplified FAFSA. EFC replaced by SAI. Number of questions reduced. FA-DDX introduced.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: Simplified FAFSA fully in effect. No major additional changes from prior year.

question_text: Does the number of people in my family still affect my financial aid the same way it used to?
ground_truth_answer: The FAFSA Simplification Act changed how family size is determined. Under the new rules, family size is based on the number of people listed as dependents on the federal tax return, rather than the prior self-reported household count. This change took effect starting with the 2024-25 FAFSA.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 2 — family size definition + 2024-25 Ch. 2 for comparison
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: Family size began transitioning to tax-return-based definition under FAFSA Simplification Act.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: Family size determined by number of dependents on the tax return. Self-reported household size no longer used for the SAI calculation.

question_text: I'm applying for aid for two kids in college at the same time — does having more than one in college help us get more aid?
ground_truth_answer: Under the 2025-26 rules, the number of family members in college no longer reduces the SAI. This is a significant change from prior years. Under the old EFC formula, having multiple students in college simultaneously reduced the EFC, increasing aid. Under the SAI formula, this discount was eliminated.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 3 — SAI and multiple college students + 2024-25 Ch. 3 — EFC multiple student adjustment
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: EFC formula divided the parental contribution by the number of family members enrolled in college, reducing each student's EFC.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: SAI formula does not divide by number of college students. Having multiple family members in college no longer reduces the SAI. Each student's SAI is calculated independently.

question_text: My family owns a small business — does that count against my financial aid?
ground_truth_answer: Under the 2025-26 rules, small businesses owned by the family with fewer than 100 full-time employees are excluded from the SAI calculation. This is the same exclusion that applied under the prior EFC formula. However, the definition of excluded assets was clarified under the FAFSA Simplification Act.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 3 — SAI asset exclusions + 2024-25 Ch. 3 — EFC asset exclusions
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: Small businesses with fewer than 100 employees excluded from EFC calculation.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: Same exclusion applies under SAI formula. Asset treatment clarified but exclusion unchanged.

question_text: Does my 529 college savings plan hurt my chances of getting financial aid?
ground_truth_answer: Yes, but to a limited degree. A 529 plan owned by a dependent student's parent is counted as a parental asset and assessed at a maximum rate of 5.64%. Under both the old EFC and new SAI formulas, parental assets are weighted more favorably than student assets. The specific assessment rate did not change substantially with the FAFSA Simplification Act.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 3 — asset treatment under SAI + 2024-25 Ch. 3 — asset treatment under EFC
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: 529 owned by parent assessed at up to 5.64% of value under EFC formula.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: 529 owned by parent assessed at up to 5.64% under SAI formula. Grandparent-owned 529 distributions no longer reported as student income starting 2024-25.

question_text: I heard there's a new way the FAFSA handles money my grandparents give me for college — what changed?
ground_truth_answer: Starting with the 2024-25 FAFSA, distributions from grandparent-owned 529 plans are no longer reported as student income on the FAFSA. Previously, these distributions were counted as student income and could significantly reduce aid eligibility. This change was part of the FAFSA Simplification Act.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 2 — income reporting changes + Ch. 3 — SAI asset and income treatment
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: Grandparent 529 distributions no longer reported as student income — first year of this change under FAFSA Simplification Act.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: Same rule in effect. Grandparent 529 distributions excluded from student income calculation.

question_text: What is the Student Aid Index and how is it different from what they used to use?
ground_truth_answer: The Student Aid Index (SAI) is a number used to determine federal aid eligibility. It replaced the Expected Family Contribution (EFC). Unlike the EFC, the SAI can be negative (as low as −1500), indicating the highest level of financial need. A lower SAI means more aid. The SAI is not the amount a family is expected to pay.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 3 — SAI definition + 2024-25 Ch. 3 — EFC definition
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: Expected Family Contribution (EFC) used. EFC is always zero or positive. Interpreted as expected parental contribution.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: Student Aid Index (SAI) replaces EFC. Can range from −1500 to 999,999. Explicitly not an expected payment amount. Calculated from tax and asset data via FA-DDX.

question_text: I filed my FAFSA last year — do I need to redo the whole thing or can I just update it?
ground_truth_answer: Students must submit a new FAFSA each academic year. However, most information carries over from the prior year's application and can be updated rather than re-entered from scratch. Tax data is refreshed automatically via the FA-DDX each year.
section_reference: FSA Handbook 2025-26 Vol. AVG Ch. 1 — FAFSA renewal process + Ch. 2 — FA-DDX annual data refresh
old_answer_2425: 2024-25: Renewal FAFSA available with prior year data carried over. IRS-DRT used to refresh tax data.
new_answer_2526: 2025-26: Renewal FAFSA available. FA-DDX replaces IRS-DRT for automatic annual tax data refresh.
